Browse by flow

Switch interview mode

4 flows
High-level design interview prep HLD
Architecture, distributed systems trade-offs, and end-to-end system walkthroughs for classic system design rounds.
Low-level design interview prep LLD
Object modeling, extensible APIs, machine-coding structure, and follow-up handling for class-design rounds.
Data structures and algorithms interview prep DSA
Curated coding-round practice across general problem sets and company-specific question banks for array, tree, graph, DP, and mock-round prep.
AI Engineer learning path AI/ML
End-to-end AI/ML engineering from foundations through LLMs, RAG, agents, MLOps, and responsible AI for building production intelligent systems.

Curriculum → High-level design interview prep → Foundations and estimation → Problem framing and requirements

Lesson 1 of 5

Problem framing and requirements

Start every design with the user journey, hard constraints, and the two or three metrics that will define success.

25-35 min HLD flow Foundations and estimation 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Strong interview answers feel structured because the requirements phase narrows the search space before any component is introduced.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. What to ask first Who are the primary actors and what is the most common request path?2. Translate goals into system constraints Capture latency SLOs per critical endpoint3. Shape the rest of the conversation Draw the critical read path and write path

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Strong interview answers feel structured because the requirements phase narrows the search space before any component is introduced. 2. What to ask first Who are the primary actors and what is the most common request path?3. Translate goals into system constraints Capture latency SLOs per critical endpoint4. Shape the rest of the conversation Draw the critical read path and write path
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Strong interview answers feel structured because the requirements phase narrows the search space before any component is introduced.

What to say first

  • Open with user-facing requirements and non-functional constraints.
  • State traffic assumptions numerically, even if they are rough.
  • Choose a baseline design before exploring optimizations.
2

Study section

What to ask first

Clarify the product surface area, core actors, read/write mix, latency expectations, consistency expectations, and what must still work during a partial outage.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Who are the primary actors and what is the most common request path?
  • What is the target peak QPS, growth rate, and geographic footprint?
  • What data absolutely cannot be lost or shown stale?
3

Study section

Translate goals into system constraints

Convert business language into engineering budgets. A chat product implies fan-out and ordering questions, while a payments product implies auditability and idempotency.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Capture latency SLOs per critical endpoint
  • Separate durability from availability requirements
  • Identify compliance, privacy, and abuse constraints early
4

Study section

Shape the rest of the conversation

State assumptions and choose one baseline architecture quickly so the discussion can move to trade-offs instead of staying abstract.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Draw the critical read path and write path
  • Call out the first likely bottleneck
  • Mention one future scaling lever you would keep available

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Open with user-facing requirements and non-functional constraints.
  • State traffic assumptions numerically, even if they are rough.
  • Choose a baseline design before exploring optimizations.
  • Explicitly call out one thing that is out of scope for the first version.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Jumping into storage or queues before the workload is clear.
  • Treating consistency, durability, and availability as the same thing.
  • Ignoring operational requirements such as moderation, analytics, or abuse prevention.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • What five questions would you ask before designing a notifications service?
  • Which requirement usually changes the architecture the most: latency, correctness, or cost? Why?
  • How would your framing change for an internal admin tool versus a global consumer app?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Back-of-the-envelope estimation Foundations and estimationAvailability, consistency, and CAP trade-offs Foundations and estimation

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Problem framing and requirements

Turn estimation and baseline trade-offs into an actual workload so you can see which assumption breaks first. Apply it to problem framing and requirements so the trade-offs feel concrete.

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Explain the concept
Ln 1, Col 1 1 lines 0 words 0.0 KB markdown

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Explain the concept

Teach "Problem framing and requirements" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • What five questions would you ask before designing a notifications service?
  • What to ask first
  • Translate goals into system constraints
  • Open with user-facing requirements and non-functional constraints.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
